27 min read
1 hour ago

--

Curated for SDE1 to SDE2 rounds in Indian product companies (F5, Piramal, Razorpay, Swiggy, Atlassian, PhonePe, Flipkart, and similar)
You already know how to program. This document does not teach you loops and variables. It teaches you the JavaScript-specific behavior that interviewers hammer on: closures, this, the event loop, prototypes, coercion, and the implement-from-scratch problems. Every topic follows: What it is → Code → Why it behaves this way → Real scenario → How the interviewer twists it.

📑 TABLE OF CONTENTS
PART 0 How to use this + the JS interview landscape
PART 1 Execution context, scope, hoisting (var / let / const, TDZ)
PART 2 CLOSURES (the single most asked topic)
PART 3 The `this` keyword + call / apply / bind
PART 4 Prototypes, prototypal inheritance, classes
PART 5 Type system: coercion, == vs ===, value vs reference, copying
PART 6 THE EVENT LOOP (call stack, microtasks, macrotasks)
PART 7 Promises + async / await
PART 8 Higher-order functions, currying, composition
PART 9 Array + object methods (map / filter / reduce), immutability
PART 10 Debounce + throttle (implement from scratch)
PART 11 DOM, events, delegation, bubbling / capturing
PART 12 Modern JS (destructuring, spread / rest, optional chaining, modules)
PART 13 Browser / web APIs (storage, fetch, timers)
PART 14 IMPLEMENT FROM SCRATCH / polyfills (machine coding round)
PART 15 Output-prediction / trick questions bank
PART 16 Rapid-fire Q&A bank
PART 17 Final checklist + quick revision plan
PART 0 · How to use this + the interview landscape
🎯 What actually gets tested in India
For SDE1 to SDE2 front-end / full-stack roles, JavaScript shows up in three flavors:
ROUND TYPE WHAT THEY TEST WEIGHT
------------------- ------------------------------------- --------
Concept + output "What does this print and why?" HIGH
closures, this, event loop, coercion Implement from Write debounce / throttle / deep clone / HIGH
scratch Promise.all / curry / bind polyfill Applied / web DOM events, fetch, async flows, MEDIUM
storage, performance🧩 The curated 20% (if you have one day, master these)
1. Closures (definition + loop trap + private state + memoize)
2. The event loop (setTimeout vs Promise ordering)
3. `this` + call / apply / bind
4. Promises + async/await (sequential vs parallel, error handling)
5. var / let / const + hoisting + TDZ
6. == vs === + coercion + truthy/falsy
7. Implement: debounce, throttle, deep clone, Promise.all, curry
8. Prototypal inheritance (chain + class sugar)If you can explain closures, the event loop, and this fluently and implement debounce cold, you clear the bar for most rounds. Everything else is depth.

PART 1 · Execution Context, Scope, Hoisting
1.1 Execution context (the mental model)
Concept. Every time JS runs code, it creates an execution context: an environment holding the variables, functions, and the value of this for that scope. There is one global context, and a new one is pushed onto the call stack for each function call.
Each context is built in two phases:
PHASE 1 - CREATION (memory / hoisting)
- var declarations → allocated, set to undefined
- function declarations → fully stored (hoisted with body)
- let / const declarations → allocated but UNINITIALIZED (TDZ) PHASE 2 - EXECUTION
- code runs line by line, assignments happenThis two-phase model is why hoisting exists. Understand this and hoisting stops being magic.
1.2 Scope and lexical scope
SCOPE TYPE CREATED BY ACCESSIBLE FROM
------------- ------------------------ -------------------------
Global outside any function everywhere
Function a function body inside that function
Block { } with let/const inside that block only
Lexical where code is WRITTEN inner sees outer, not viceLexical scoping means a function’s access to variables is determined by where it is defined in the source, not where it is called. This is the foundation of closures (Part 2).
1.3 var vs let vs const (guaranteed question)
FEATURE var let const
--------------- ----------------- -------------- --------------
Scope function block block
Hoisting yes → undefined yes → TDZ yes → TDZ
Re-declaration allowed not allowed not allowed
Re-assignment allowed allowed not allowed
Global property attaches to window no noconsole.log(a); // undefined (var hoisted, initialized to undefined)
var a = 10;console.log(b); // ReferenceError: Cannot access 'b' before initialization
let b = 20; // b is hoisted but in the Temporal Dead Zone1.4 The Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)
Concept. let and const ARE hoisted, but they are not initialized. The window between the start of the block and the actual declaration line is the TDZ. Accessing the variable there throws a ReferenceError.
{ ────────────────────────── block starts, TDZ begins for x
// console.log(x) here → ReferenceError
let x = 5; ─────────────── TDZ ends, x initialized
// x usable here
}Interview one-liner. “let and const are hoisted just like var, but they stay uninitialized in the Temporal Dead Zone until their declaration runs, so accessing them early throws instead of returning undefined. var is initialized to undefined immediately, which is why it does not throw."
1.5 Function vs variable hoisting (output trap)
foo(); // "I am foo" → function declarations hoist fully
function foo() { console.log("I am foo"); }bar(); // TypeError: bar is not a function
var bar = function () { console.log("I am bar"); };
// var bar is hoisted as undefined; the assignment happens laterWhy. Function declarations are hoisted with their entire body. Function expressions assigned to var only hoist the variable (as undefined), so calling it before assignment fails.

PART 2 · CLOSURES (the single most asked topic)
If you nail one topic, make it this. Closures appear in theory, output questions, and implement-from-scratch (debounce, memoize, once all use closures).
2.1 What is a closure?
Concept. A closure is a function bundled together with references to the variables from its surrounding (lexical) scope. Even after the outer function has returned, the inner function still “remembers” and can access those variables.
function outer() {
let count = 0; // lives in outer's scope
return function inner() {
count++; // inner closes over count
return count;
};
}
const counter = outer(); // outer() has RETURNED
counter(); // 1
counter(); // 2 ← count survived because inner keeps a reference to itouter() runs, creates count = 0
│
└─ returns inner (inner carries a reference to outer's scope)
│
outer's context would normally be garbage-collected...
│
but inner still points at `count`, so it STAYS ALIVE
│
each counter() call mutates the same preserved countInterview one-liner. “A closure is a function plus the lexical environment it was created in. The inner function keeps a live reference to the outer variables, so they persist even after the outer function returns. This is how JavaScript gives us private state and stateful functions.”
2.2 The classic loop trap (the most famous JS question)
// ❌ prints 3, 3, 3
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 100);
}// ✅ prints 0, 1, 2
for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 100);
}Why. var is function-scoped, so all three callbacks close over the same single i. By the time the timeouts fire (after the loop finishes), i is 3. let is block-scoped, so each iteration creates a fresh i, and each callback closes over its own copy.
var → one shared box [ i ] ← all 3 callbacks point here → final value 3
let → box[0]=0 box[1]=1 box[2]=2 ← each callback has its own boxThe pre-let fix (they may ask): wrap in an IIFE to create a new scope per iteration.
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
(function (j) { setTimeout(() => console.log(j), 100); })(i);
}2.3 Real use cases (say these to prove understanding)
1. Data privacy / encapsulation (module pattern):
function createBankAccount(initial) {
let balance = initial; // truly private, no outside access
return {
deposit: (amt) => (balance += amt),
withdraw: (amt) => (balance -= amt),
getBalance: () => balance,
};
}
const acc = createBankAccount(100);
acc.deposit(50); // 150
acc.balance; // undefined ← cannot touch it directly2. Function factories (parameterized functions):
const multiplier = (factor) => (num) => num * factor;
const double = multiplier(2); // closes over factor = 2
double(5); // 103. Memoization, debounce, throttle, once all rely on closures to hold private cache/timer/flag state (see Parts 10 and 14).
Interview twist — “Do closures cause memory leaks?” They can, if a closure holds references to large objects longer than needed (e.g., a forgotten event listener closing over a big DOM subtree). The variables stay in memory because the closure references them. Fix by removing listeners and nulling references when done.

PART 3 · The this Keyword + call / apply / bind
this is the second most misunderstood topic. The value of this is decided by how a function is called, not where it is defined (except arrow functions).
3.1 The four binding rules (in priority order)
RULE HOW CALLED this REFERS TO
--------------- ----------------------------- ----------------------
1. new binding new Foo() the new object
2. explicit fn.call/apply/bind(obj) the passed obj
3. implicit obj.method() obj (left of the dot)
4. default fn() (standalone) global / undefined
(undefined in strict mode)const user = {
name: "PSP",
greet() { return `Hi ${this.name}`; },
};
user.greet(); // "Hi PSP" → implicit: this = userconst fn = user.greet;
fn(); // "Hi undefined" → default: this = global/undefined
// ❗ lost binding: the dot is goneThe “lost this” trap above is extremely common. Passing a method as a callback (setTimeout(user.greet)) detaches it from its object. Fix with bind or an arrow wrapper.
3.2 Arrow functions and this (critical distinction)
Concept. Arrow functions do not have their own this. They inherit this lexically from the enclosing scope at definition time. They also cannot be used with new and have no arguments object.
const obj = {
name: "PSP",
regular() {
setTimeout(function () { console.log(this.name); }, 0); // undefined (default binding)
setTimeout(() => console.log(this.name), 0); // "PSP" (lexical this = obj)
},
};regular function callback → gets its OWN this (default = global/undefined)
arrow function callback → no own this, borrows regular()'s this = objInterview one-liner. “Regular functions get this from how they are called; arrow functions capture this from where they are written. That is why arrows are the go-to for callbacks and array methods inside objects, and why you never use an arrow as an object method or constructor."
3.3 call, apply, bind (know the differences + implement bind)
METHOD INVOKES? ARGUMENTS FORMAT RETURNS
------- -------- ----------------------- --------------------
call yes now comma-separated list the result
apply yes now single array of args the result
bind no, later comma-separated list a NEW bound functionfunction intro(city, role) { return `${this.name}, ${role}, ${city}`; }
const person = { name: "PSP" };intro.call(person, "Bangalore", "SDE"); // invokes now, args listed
intro.apply(person, ["Bangalore", "SDE"]); // invokes now, args as array
const bound = intro.bind(person, "Bangalore"); // returns fn, partially applied
bound("SDE"); // "PSP, SDE, Bangalore"Memory hook: call = Comma, apply = Array, bind = Bound-for-later.
Real scenario for bind. Passing an object method as an event handler in React class components or setTimeout: setTimeout(user.greet.bind(user), 1000) preserves this. Also partial application (pre-filling arguments).

PART 4 · Prototypes, Prototypal Inheritance, Classes
4.1 The prototype chain
Concept. Every JavaScript object has an internal link to another object called its prototype. When you access a property, JS looks on the object itself; if not found, it walks up the prototype chain until it finds it or hits null. This is how inheritance works in JS (no real classes underneath).
myArray ──▶ Array.prototype ──▶ Object.prototype ──▶ null
